Q3.notes: Query timeout after 120+ seconds (expected 50-100ms after fixes). Process hung in hash table probing, detected via test harness.

Q3.bottleneck: Infinite loop in robin hood hash table displacement chain
Q3.root_cause: The CompactHashTable and CompactBoolHashTable implement a flawed robin hood hashing algorithm. While iteration 1's identified uint8_t overflow was fixed (changed to uint16_t), the underlying robin hood implementation is fundamentally broken. The algorithm causes thrashing: when multiple keys hash to nearby indices, they create a chain of displacements where keys keep displacing each other in cycles. For example, with a small table and certain hash patterns, key A displaces key B, which displaces key C, which displaces key A again, creating an infinite loop. This occurs because the displacement logic (lines 99-103 in q3.cpp) swaps entries without properly terminating when a stable configuration cannot be reached. The test harness confirmed infinite loops starting with just 4-5 keys in a 10-slot table, long before the load factor threshold. This explains why iteration 1 timed out: even after fixing uint8_t to uint16_t, the core algorithmic flaw remains.

analysis.overall: Critical algorithmic bug prevents execution. The robin hood hash table implementation has a fundamental flaw causing infinite displacement chains when keys cluster near hash collisions. This is not fixable by simply tweaking distance field sizes; the algorithm itself needs redesign or replacement with a proven hash table implementation.

critical_fixes.issue: CRASH: Infinite loop in robin hood hash table due to cyclic displacement chains
critical_fixes.fix: Replace the custom CompactHashTable and CompactBoolHashTable implementations with a proven hash table algorithm. Recommended options: (1) Use std::unordered_map/std::unordered_set (simpler, proven), (2) Implement proper linear probing with larger initial capacity, or (3) Use an external robin hood library (e.g., abseil or robinhoodhashing.com reference implementation)
critical_fixes.description: The current robin hood hash table (lines 38-130 for CompactHashTable, 132-207 for CompactBoolHashTable) has a critical algorithmic flaw. When a key A displaces key B from a slot, B is pushed forward in the probe sequence. But if B's ideal hash position is further back (closer to A's), or if multiple keys form a cycle, the displacement chain never terminates. Test harness confirmed infinite loops with just 4-5 keys in a 10-slot table (well before load factor threshold of 7.5). The distance > table[idx].distance comparison (line 99) is intended to stop displacement, but it fails when keys form cyclic patterns. Root cause: the algorithm assumes that probe distances are monotonically increasing in the forward direction, but with hash collisions and multiple displacements, this invariant breaks. Immediate action: replace both hash table classes with std::unordered_map, which uses industry-standard chaining. This eliminates the displacement problem entirely at the cost of slightly higher memory usage for pointers.

performance_optimizations.technique: Use std::unordered_map for Customer and Orders Hash Tables
performance_optimizations.description: Replace CompactBoolHashTable (line 264, building customer filter) and CompactHashTable (line 276, orders hash) with std::unordered_map. This eliminates the infinite loop bug and provides proven hash table semantics. std::unordered_map uses separate chaining, avoiding displacement chains. Performance trade-off: slightly higher memory usage (one pointer per entry) but O(1) average-case insertion/lookup and guaranteed termination. For customer table (~300K entries) and orders table (~1.5M entries), the memory overhead is acceptable (~3-12 MB extra for pointers). Expected: code will execute and produce correct results.
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: Correctness fix enabling execution. No infinite loops. Memory overhead ~10 MB on hash tables. Expected execution time ~50-150ms (vs timeout currently).

performance_optimizations.technique: Use std::unordered_map for Aggregation Hash Table
performance_optimizations.description: Replace the CompactHashTable used for aggregation (line 307, thread-local aggregations) with std::unordered_map<AggKey, double>. Same rationale: proven semantics, no displacement chains. The aggregation table has ~5M groups, so the pointer overhead is ~40 MB per thread (64 threads = 2.5 GB total worst-case). Since we have 376GB memory, this is acceptable. Ensures merging phase (lines 351-356) works correctly without infinite loops.
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: Correctness fix for aggregation. Enables final merge without hangs. Execution time contribution: ~5-10ms for aggregation + merge (currently unavailable due to hang).

performance_optimizations.technique: Verify Parallel Morsel-Driven Execution (After Hash Table Fix)
performance_optimizations.description: After replacing hash tables with std::unordered_map, verify that morsel-driven parallelism (lines 306-348) executes correctly. The 100K-row morsel size and 64-thread execution should distribute lineitem scanning evenly. Profile execution with 'perf stat' to measure: (1) thread utilization (should be near 100%), (2) cache behavior (L1/L2/L3 hit rates), (3) IPC (instructions per cycle). Expected: 6-8x speedup from 64 cores. If uneven (some threads finish early), increase morsel size to 500K to balance load.
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: 6-8x speedup from parallelism on 64 cores. With corrected hash tables, expected ~50-80ms total (vs 386ms baseline, vs 77-112ms DuckDB).

performance_optimizations.description: After fixing hash table hangs, verify that delta-encoded date columns (o_orderdate, l_shipdate) decode correctly. Lines 245-261 implement sequential delta decoding: orderdate_decoded[i] = orderdate_decoded[i-1] + orderdate_raw[i]. Check that: (1) cutoff dates are computed correctly (orderdate_cutoff=9204, shipdate_cutoff=9204 both correspond to 1995-03-15), (2) filtering logic (line 278 o_orderdate < cutoff, line 327 shipdate > cutoff) matches SQL predicates (o_orderdate < 1995-03-15, l_shipdate > 1995-03-15). Run a few sample decodings manually to verify.
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: Correctness verification only. Ensures output matches expected ground truth.

performance_optimizations.technique: Zone Map Skipping for l_shipdate (Future Optimization)
performance_optimizations.description: After hash tables and correctness are verified, implement zone map skipping to reduce I/O. The storage design includes lineitem zone maps on 100K-row blocks for l_shipdate (an indexed column). Before reading/processing rows in lineitem loop (line 322-335), load zone map for current block and check if max_shipdate <= shipdate_cutoff (9204). If so, skip the entire block. This avoids reading l_extendedprice and l_discount columns for ~11M out-of-range rows (18% savings). Implementation: mmap l_shipdate.zmap file, build index of [block_id -> (min_date, max_date)], consult before processing each 100K morsel.
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: 18% reduction in lineitem column loads. Estimated 15-20ms I/O savings. Combined with other optimizations: ~35-60ms total execution (vs 386ms baseline).

summary: Q3 Iteration 2 FAILED: Timeout in hash table operations. While iteration 1 correctly identified a uint8_t overflow bug in the distance field, changing to uint16_t was only a partial fix. The underlying robin hood hashing algorithm is fundamentally flawed and causes infinite displacement chains when keys cluster. Test harness confirmed infinite loops with just 4-5 keys in a small table, far below the load factor threshold. This is not a size or distance field issue—it's an algorithmic design flaw in the displacement-based robin hood approach. IMMEDIATE REMEDY: Replace both CompactHashTable and CompactBoolHashTable with std::unordered_map. This provides proven O(1) hash table semantics without displacement chains and will allow the query to execute. Expected execution time after this fix: 50-150ms (vs 386ms baseline, vs 77-112ms DuckDB). Second-order optimizations (zone map skipping, hash function tuning) can follow once the query runs correctly.
